title: Persistent Crises and Levered Asset Prices
abstract: This paper shows that standard disaster risk models are inconsistent with movements in stock market volatility and credit spreads during disasters. We resolve this shortcoming by incorporating persistent macroeconomic crises into a structural credit risk model. The model successfully captures the joint dynamics of aggregate consumption, financial leverage, and asset market risks, both unconditionally and during crises. Leverage strongly amplifies fundamental shocks by continuing to rise while crises endure. We structurally estimate the model and show that it replicates the firm-level implied volatility curve and its cross-sectional relation with observable proxies of default risk.

article.1. Model:
JEL G12 - Asset Pricing; Trading volume; Bond Interest Rates G13 - Contingent Pricing; Futures Pricing G33 - Bankruptcy; Liquidation Issue Section: Article Editor: Stefano Giglio Stefano Giglio Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Macroeconomic crises typically last for multiple years and coincide with large increases in financial leverage and surging risks in financial markets.Figure 1exemplifies these features based on the Great Depression. Between 1930 and 1933, aggregate consumption dropped by 16.6%⁠, firms’ leverage ratios more than doubled, stock return volatility tripled, and credit spreads increased sixfold. Additionally, the four series display a striking degree of comovement and reach simultaneous peaks or troughs in the third year of the depression.1

Figure 1 Consumption and asset prices during the Great Depression Aggregate consumption and firms’ leverage ratios, return volatilities, and credit spreads are plotted in an event window around the Great Depression. Firm moments are averaged across the 100 largest firms. Solid lines and bars correspond to U.S. data. Dotted lines represent model data, averaged across simulated crises that lead to cumulative consumption drops of 16.6 % ⁠ , like the Great Depression. Dashed lines represent analogous results for the time-varying disaster risk models of Wachter (2013) and Seo and Wachter (2018). Section 3 discusses details on the data and model simulations. Surprisingly, standard disaster risk models fail to capture asset market risks during crises. We illustrate this shortcoming for the models ofWachter (2013)andSeo and Wachter (2018), which were explicitly designed to explain thedynamicsof equity volatility and credit spreads, respectively.Figure 1shows that conditional risks in debt and equity markets remain essentially unchanged during simulated crises in these models, whereas they increase sharply in the data. There are two reasons. First, both models assume that disasters are instantaneous IID events, which implies that they lead to permanent drops in asset values without affecting conditional cash flow risks. Second, the models do not take into account the effect of default risk on equity valuations. Higher leverage ratios resulting from adverse shocks therefore do not make equity riskier. Importantly, both features are shared by other popular disaster risk models, including those ofRietz (1988),Barro (2006), andGabaix (2012).

In this paper, we propose and estimate a model that captures the joint dynamics of aggregate consumption, asset prices, and financial leverage, both unconditionally and during crises. To do so, we incorporate persistent macroeconomic crises into a structural credit risk model. The model features a representative agent with recursive preferences and a regime-switching process for aggregate consumption and firms’ cash flows. Firms choose leverage ratios by weighing the tax benefits of debt against the costs arising from bankruptcy. They optimally issue more debt when their leverage ratios are too low and default when their leverage ratios are too high. When the economy switches into the crisis regime, consumption and earnings growth are persistently depressed and more volatile. The onset of a crisis is characterized by a decrease in asset values, and an increase in financial leverage and risk premiums. As the crisis continues to unfold, equity values continue to fall and leverage continues to rise, further increasing risk premiums and measures of equity and default risk. This mechanism allows the model to replicate the dynamics of leverage and asset prices during crises, as the dotted lines inFigure 1illustrate. Hence, endogenous financial leverage provides an important amplification mechanism for fundamental shocks during crises.

To quantify the effect of leverage on valuations, we estimate the model based on data for S&P 100 firms over 2004 to 2019 with the Simulated Method of Moments. The estimation targets a detailed set of firm-level moments, including means and volatilities of leverage ratios, stock returns, CDS rates, and the level and skew of option-implied volatilities. These moments allow us to identify, among others, the cyclicality of idiosyncratic and aggregate cash flow risk, the level and cyclicality of bankruptcy costs, as well as risk aversion of the representative investor. Importantly, the estimation does not rely on financial market data for the Great Depression period. The model’s ability to capture asset market risks during disasters (shown inFigure 1) is therefore not mechanical. Instead, it can be interpreted as an external validation of the proposed mechanism.

While the corporate finance literature has provided evidence in favor of countercyclical loss rates on bonds, we find that bankruptcy costs are strongly countercyclical as well. Gauging the time-varying nature of bankruptcy costs requires a structural model because empirical loss rates are driven by time variation in both asset values and bankruptcy costs. As bankruptcy costs comove with the pricing kernel, their cyclicality drives a wedge between expected bankruptcy costs under the physical and risk-neutral measure, which amounts to a risk premium. In particular, average risk-neutral bankruptcy costs are 37.6%⁠, compared to 21.6%under the physical measure. This evidence suggests that structural estimations with risk-neutral agents, as is common in corporate finance models, may result in biased estimates. Our exercise therefore highlights the importance of risk adjustments in structural corporate finance models.

Persistent crises and endogenous leverage enable the model to explain risk premiums across equity, credit, and option markets. First, the model generates a low and smooth risk-free rate and a realistic (levered) equity premium for an estimated risk aversion coefficient of about 8. Second, it resolves the credit spread puzzle by generating high credit spreads despite low leverage and low physical default probabilities. The intuitive reason is that defaults cluster in macroeconomic crises, where investors’ marginal utility is very high. We show that, when consumption growth is calibrated as in the long-run risks literature instead, the model is unable to jointly match leverage ratios and credit spreads of S&P 100 firms, even though we reestimate parameters to best match these moments. Third, the model provides a good match for the shape and time variation of the implied volatility curve. In this regard, it resembles models with time-varying instantaneous disaster risk, such asGabaix (2012)andWachter (2013), which are consistent with the IV curve for index options. Different from this prior work, however, we focus on the pricing of options on individual firms and provide a framework that captures the cross-sectional relation between financial leverage and the implied volatility curve.

As in the data set ofBarro and Ursua (2012), our model features crises with heterogenous duration and severity, where longer lasting crises are more severe on average. In fact, our consumption process captures the entire joint distribution of crisis magnitudes and durations. Longer-lasting crises tend to be associated with larger run-ups in leverage, equity volatility and credit spreads (shown inFigure 1). In contrast, models with instantaneous disasters and no financial leverage imply that the realized crisis magnitude has no effect on conditional asset prices. Another attractive feature of our consumption process is that it is able to generate finite samples which mimic the postwar U.S. sample. The same is not true for the long-run risks framework, as shown byBeeler and Campbell (2012).

Our model implies that equity options are sensitive to credit risk because they represent compound options on a firm’s assets. To test this implication, we sort firms based on their distance-to-default. We find in the data that increased default risk is associated with significantly higher levels of option-implied volatilities and steeper implied volatility curves. The first finding is intuitive because default risk reflects a combination of asset volatility and financial leverage, both of which make stock returns more volatile. The second finding arises because default risk leads to more conditional left skewness, as equity holders’ return equals−100%in default. Our model replicates both patterns quantitatively very well, even though these moments are not targeted in the estimation.

We followNakamura et al. (2013)in modeling disasters as a persistent Markov chain regime. Their model abstracts from financial leverage and aims to explain the equity premium, while we target firm-level moments, including option prices and CDS rates, which are directly linked to leverage.Gourio (2012)explores the business cycle implications ofNakamura et al.’s (2013) disaster specification when applied to firms’ total factor productivity. In contemporaneous work to ours,Ghaderi, Kilic, and Seo (2022)propose an alternative way to model long-lasting disasters. Their disaster process features an observable time-varying probability whose long-run mean is itself time-varying and unobservable to economic agents. Similar to the leverage channel we propose, learning about the long-run mean allows their model to capture the fact that conditional asset prices change slowly during disasters. While they study the dynamics of aggregate asset prices, such as index options, we focus on the pricing of firm-level options and credit default swaps. As such, the two mechanisms might complement each other empirically.

FollowingAbel (1999), most consumption-based asset pricing models capture leverage in a reduced form way by assuming that firms’ cash flows are related to consumption viaDt=Ctϕ⁠, with a “leverage” parameterϕ>1⁠. This functional form captures the empirical fact that cash flows are more volatile than consumption. However, it does not allow leverage to change over time in response to changing equity valuations—a key feature of our model. In contrast toAbel (1999),Gabaix (2012)proposes an asset pricing model with time-varying recovery rates on equity, which can alternatively be interpreted as time-varying leverage. It appears feasible to extend Gabaix’s model to account for the comovement between leverage and asset market risks by making shocks to recovery rates correlated with the disaster event. While this reduced-form approach would capture the right mechanism, our contribution is to provide a micro foundation for leverage dynamics during crises.

Another way to make asset prices more risky after a disaster realization is to assume that disasters are self-exciting, as inNowotny (2011). Different from IID disasters, this mechanism captures the fact that stock market volatility rises at the onset of a disaster. Our mechanism captures the additional fact that credit spreads tend to rise slowly and in tandem with leverage over the course of a crises.

We also closely build on structural credit risk models, such asHackbarth, Miao, and Morellec (2006),Chen, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2009),Bhamra, Kuehn, and Strebulaev (2010), andChen (2010), by modeling capital structure decisions at the firm level. Different from these papers, we structurally estimate our model and rely on macroeconomic disasters as the primary driver of risk premiums. The latter is crucial in our setting because the low leverage of S&P 100 firms (of around 19%⁠) makes the credit spread puzzle particularly pronounced: When we reestimate the model based on a long-run risks pricing kernel, as inBhamra, Kuehn, and Strebulaev (2010), andChen (2010), we find that it is unable to jointly match the leverage ratios and CDS rates of S&P 100 firms.2

One of the first papers to explain credit spreads in a disaster risk model isGourio (2013). He augments a standard real business cycle model with instantaneous disasters in total factor productivity and risky single-period debt, and relates credit spreads to firms’ investment decisions. We cannot speak to this relationship because our model abstracts from investment. The other main difference to our model lies in the way capital structure decisions are modelled. First, leverage does not change following disaster realizations in Gourio’s model because firms adjust their capital structures every period. As a result, default risk, return volatility, and credit spreads do not change when disasters materialize. Second, Gourio’s model abstracts from heterogeneity in firms’ capital structures and therefore cannot speak to the cross-sectional relation between leverage, default risk, and asset prices. Third, the credit spread puzzle is much more pronounced in our setting because S&P 100 firms have low average leverage ratios of around 20%⁠, whereas Gourio’s model is calibrated to average leverage of 55%⁠.

While many consumption-based models have considered the pricing of index options (for instance,Gabaix,2012), this literature has thus far ignored the pricing of single-name equity options and the implications of financial leverage for option prices. However, we are not the first to explore the link between credit risk and option pricing. Prior literature has recognized that option prices contain information about the dynamics and pricing of risk that is useful for the valuation of credit instruments.3Two closely related papers areCollin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Yang (2012)andSeo and Wachter (2018). Both studies propose structural credit risk models with disaster risk and use them to price index options and CDX contracts, that is, market-level debt and equity derivatives. A limitation of their frameworks is that they model asset risk instead of modeling cash flow risk to shareholders and bondholders separately. In their models, default thresholds therefore have to be specified exogenously and equity values do not approach zero when firms default. In contrast, we model optimal capital structure and default decisions, which allows us to price derivatives that are written on individual firms’ debt and equity.
The goal of our model is to capture the joint dynamics of firms’ debt and equity values, and of derivatives that are written on them. To that end, we combine a consumption-based pricing kernel with a dynamic capital structure model. We describe the pricing kernel and consumption process in Section1.1and firms’ capital structure decisions in Sections1.2–1.5. Details for the CDS valuation can be found in AppendixCand for the option valuation in AppendixD.

1.1 Pricing kernel

To allow risk premiums to fluctuate with macroeconomic conditions, we follow the consumption-based asset pricing literature and model the pricing kernel as the marginal utility of consumption of a representative investor. Specifically, the logarithm of aggregate consumption growth, g c , t + 1 ⁠ , follows a Markov-switching modulated random walk, g c , t + 1 = μ c , t + σ c , t ε c , t + 1 , (1) where the conditional mean μ c , t and volatility σ c , t of consumption growth depend on an aggregate Markov state ξ t ⁠ , and ε c , t + 1 are standard normal innovations. The aggregate state ξ t follows a Markov chain with transition matrix P ⁠ , which is independent of all other shocks in the economy.

The representative agent has recursive Epstein and Zin (1989) preferences over consumption such that the pricing kernel is given by M t , t + 1 = β θ ( λ t + 1 c + 1 λ t c ) − ( 1 − θ ) e − γ g c , t + 1 , (2) where β is the time discount rate, γ denotes relative risk aversion, ψ the elasticity of intertemporal substitution (EIS), and θ = 1 − γ 1 − 1 / ψ ⁠ . The wealth-consumption ratio λ t c follows from the Euler equation for consumption and solves ( λ t c ) θ = β θ e ( 1 − γ ) μ c , t + 1 2 ( 1 − γ ) 2 σ c , t 2 E t [ ( λ t + 1 c + 1 ) θ ] . (3)

Theh-period risk-free rate is given byRt,t+hf=1/Et[Mt,t+h]⁠, whereMt,t+h=∏i=1hMt+i−1,t+i⁠.

We assume a three-state Markov chain for the aggregate state, where drift and volatility of the logarithm of consumption growth are given by μ c = [ μ c ( 1 ) μ c ( 2 ) μ c ( 3 ) ] σ c = [ σ c ( 1 ) σ c ( 2 ) σ c ( 3 ) ] . (4)

1.2 Asset value

Firm i ’s earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) E i , t are exogenous and follow a Markov-switching modulated random walk, whose conditional growth rate and volatility depend on the aggregate Markov state ξ t ⁠ . Specifically, the logarithm of earnings growth, g i , t + 1 ⁠ , follows g i , t + 1 = μ t + σ t ε t + 1 + ζ t ν i , t + 1 , (6) where ν i , t + 1 is an idiosyncratic standard normal shock that is independent across time and firms, and ε t + 1 is an aggregate standard normal shock that is independent across time and has correlation ρ with the consumption shock ε c , t + 1 ⁠ .

We assume that the conditional moments of earnings growth ( 6 ) are governed by the aggregate state, ξ t ⁠ , so that earnings and consumption are subject to the same aggregate trends. In particular, the earnings drift is parameterized as μ t = E [ μ c , t ] + ϕ μ ( μ c , t − E [ μ c , t ] ) , (7) which implies that earnings and consumption grow at the same long-run rate E [ μ c , t ] and, for ϕ μ > 0 ⁠ , that earnings growth is low when the macroeconomy is doing poorly. For ϕ μ > 1 ⁠ , the conditional growth rate of earnings varies more than that of consumption, similar to how Bansal and Yaron (2004) scale the long-run risk factor in the dividend process of their model.

We also assume that the conditional volatility of systematic cash flow risk varies with the aggregate state according to σ t = ϕ σ , 0 + ϕ σ , 1 ( σ c , t − E [ σ c , t ] ) , (8) where ϕ σ , 0 measures the level and ϕ σ , 1 the cyclicality of aggregate earnings risk. For ϕ σ , 0 > E [ σ c , t ] ⁠ , aggregate earnings are more volatile than aggregate consumption, which captures sources of operating leverage, such as sticky wages. For ϕ σ , 1 > 1 ⁠ , the volatility of aggregate earnings fluctuates more than the volatility of aggregate consumption, similar to how Bansal and Yaron (2004) scale long-run volatility risk in the dividend process of their model.

Lastly, idiosyncratic cash flow risk is parameterized as an affine function of consumption volatility, ζ t = ϕ ζ , 0 + ϕ ζ , 1 ( σ c , t − E [ σ c , t ] ) , (9) where ϕ ζ , 0 measures the level and ϕ ζ , 1 the cyclicality of idiosyncratic earnings risk. While idiosyncratic shocks are firm-specific, our parameterization of ζ t implies that their volatility is time-varying and common across firms. Such common idiosyncratic volatility in earnings growth is a robust feature of the data, as recently documented by Herskovic et al. (2016) .

The unlevered firm value is the present value of after-tax earnings. The tax environment consists of three tax rates: constant tax rate τ i on personal interest income, τ e on equity income, and τ c on corporate earnings. The after-tax cum-earnings asset value A i , t is given by A i , t = ( 1 − τ c ) ( 1 − τ e ) E i , t + E t [ M t , t + 1 A i , t + 1 ] . (10)

1.3 Debt

Firms issue perpetual debt to take advantage of the tax benefits of debt financing. Optimal leverage is determined by the trade-off between tax shields and bankruptcy costs. In addition, firms can issue more debt in the future or declare default, but they cannot retire debt to avoid default.4

We define the interest coverage ratio, κ i , t ⁠ , as the ratio of current earnings E i , t to coupon payments c i , s based on the most recent debt issuance date s κ i , t = E i , t c i , s .

When firms are hit by a sequence of negative earning shocks, earnings might not be sufficient to cover interest expenses. Eventually, the interest coverage ratio falls below the aggregate state-dependent thresholdκtD=κD(ξt)and equity holders optimally declare default.

In the opposite case, firms receive a sequence of positive news, and the interest coverage ratio rises. A rising interest coverage ratio implies lower leverage and shrinking tax shields. Importantly, firms do not refinance every period because of proportional debt issuance costs. Both issuance and target interest coverage ratios are set to maximize total firm value.

article.2. Estimation:
In this section, we describe data sources and explain how we obtain parameter values for the quantitative evaluation of the model. The model is solved at a monthly frequency. The process for aggregate consumption is calibrated to U.S. data over the past century. Firm-level parameters are estimated with the simulated method of moments based on 2004–2019 data. Because the model has to be solved numerically, an estimation of all model parameters is computationally infeasible. For the estimation, we therefore zero in on those parameters for which the existing literature provides only weak priors, namely, the parameters associated with firms’ cash flow risk, the level and cyclicality of bankruptcy costs, and risk aversion. The other parameters are based on values in the prior literature.

2.1 Consumption dynamics

Consumption is calibrated based on the sum of real nondurable and services consumption per capita from the BEA.Table 1summarizes the calibration and shows associated moments.

FollowingBarro (2006), disaster models are typically calibrated based on international data from observed macroeconomic crises. The data set ofBarro and Ursua (2012)features 125 consumption declines of at least 10%in 28 countries, dating back as far as 1870 and ending in 2009. In this data set, disasters have an average duration of 3.7 years (44 months), are associated with an average drop in total consumption expenditures of 21.6%⁠, and account for 13.3%of all years.6The Great Depression in the United States is representative of these events, with a drop in total consumption expenditures of 20.8%over 44 months (August 1929 to March 1933). Because the Great Depression accounts for only 4.1%of the years in the BEA sample period, however, it would be unreasonable to assume that investors expect the U.S. economy to spend 13.3%of the time in a severe macroeconomic crisis. Another challenge associated with international data is that they are only available for total consumption expenditures, whereas asset pricing models are typically calibrated based on nondurables and services consumption because this measure corresponds more closely to the concept of consumption in the models.

We calibrate state 3 of the Markov chain based on U.S. data to overcome these challenges.p3is set so that the average crisis lasts 44 months, like the Great Depression.πimplies a crisis frequency of 4.1%⁠, which is the value for U.S. data. This crisis frequency is conservative relative to the one observed in international data. However, one cannot reliably estimate the subjective frequency of investors in U.S. asset markets. It is therefore admittedly possible that 4.1%overestimates the true disaster probability. Similar to our choice,Barro (2006)calibrates the disaster probability in his model the empirical probability ofenteringa crisis, which equals 3.6%⁠, rather than the probability ofbeingin a crisis, which equals 13.3%⁠.μc(3)is set to generate a consumption drop of 16.58%for the average crisis, which equals the drop in U.S. nondurables and services consumption during the Great Depression. Lastly,σc(3)is estimated based on the sample standard deviation of annual log consumption growth rates during the Great Depression.7

Figure 2shows the distributions of crisis durations and cumulative consumption drops for our calibration. Many crises are short lived. Half of all crisis episodes last fewer than 33 months and are associated with only moderate declines in consumption. However, crises with durations above 4 years and cumulative consumption declines of 16%or more, as experienced in the United States during the Great Depression, have sizable probabilities. For comparison, the figure also shows the distributions of crises durations and magnitudes in the international data ofBarro and Ursua (2012). Our calibration matches both distributions well. The bottom panel shows average consumption losses as a function of the crisis duration. The model captures the fact that longer crises result in larger consumption losses on average.8

One data feature our consumption process cannot account for is the existence of short-lived but very extreme crises. For example, the COVID-19 pandemic resulted in a consumption drop of nearly 13%in the first 6 months of 2020 in the United States, of which 11%materialized in Q2-2020 alone. Such an event has a near zero probability in our model because consumption losses during crises average only about 5%per year, and are normally-distributed with a fairly low standard deviation. To account for extreme short-lived crises, one could enrich the model with an additional crisis state that features an extreme negative drift and low persistence. We abstract from this additional feature to keep the model parsimonious.

We calibrate the noncrisis states 1 and 2 of the Markov chain based on the postwar period from 1947 to 2006. Data starting in 2007 are excluded from this calibration because whether the financial crisis of 2007 and 2008 is best characterized as a brief disaster episode or simply as a severe recession is unclear. The noncrisis parameters(μc(1),μc(2),σc(1),σc(2),p)are chosen such that simulated model data from states 1 and 2 perfectly matches the mean, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis, and first-order autocorrelation of annual (time-aggregated) log consumption growth over 1947 to 2006, as shown inTable 1.

2.2 Firm-level data

The estimation of firm-level parameters relies on data from a number of sources. Daily data on credit default swaps (CDS) for the period from January 2004 to June 2019 are obtained from ICE Data Services (formerly known as Credit Market Analysis Ltd. [CMA]). The data set contains information on pricing (bid and ask quotes) and contract terms of the underlying debt and credit default swaps (e.g., currency, debt seniority, credit event of restructuring, and tenor of the CDS contract).Mayordomo, Pena, and Schwartz (2010)find that the CMA database leads the price discovery process in comparison with other CDS databases, including Markit. We focus on five-year credit spreads, that is, the most actively traded CDS tenor.

Because we are interested in the joint behavior of option and CDS contracts, the empirical analysis focuses on constituents of the S&P 100 index, an index of blue-chip companies across multiple industry groups that requires constituents to have listed stock options. As is standard, we exclude regulated utilities (SIC codes between 4,900 and 4,999) and financial firms (SIC codes between 6,000 and 6,999) because they have a nonstandard capital structure.

Equity option data come from the Chicago Board Options Exchange (CBOE), which provides end-of-day bid and ask quotes on traded put and call options and associated implied volatilities. Individual equity options are American style, and the CBOE uses binomial trees to compute implied volatilities that account for early exercise. Since the available set of maturity and strike price combinations varies across both time and firms, we use a Gaussian kernel to interpolate implied volatilities on every day to a constant maturity of 30 calendar days and a fine grid of strike prices.9Our analysis is therefore based on options data that are homogeneous across firms.

We retain observations on CDS contracts with no restructuring (XR) or modified restructuring clause (MR), on senior debt, denominated in U.S. dollars. After applying these filters and merging with the CBOE data, we are left with a sample of 99 unique firms with at least 10 monthly observations, covering a total of 11,435 firm-month observations over the period from 2004 to 2019.

Daily mid-quotes of the CDS contracts are averaged within a calendar month to obtain a time series of monthly average credit default swap rates for each firm. Over the entire sample period, the average firm’s CDS rate equals 61 basis points (bps), with considerable variation both across firms and within firms over time.

We summarize the cross-section of option prices for each firm with the level and slope of its IV curve at the 1-month maturity. The level is measured by the at-the-money IV, which we compute by linearly interpolating the IV of out-of-the money call and put options with strike prices closest to the current stock price. The slope, commonly referred to as IV skew, is estimated by the difference between the IV of a put option that is one-standard-deviation out-of-the-money (using the at-the-money IV to measure standard deviations) and the at-the-money IV. We then average the daily estimates for each firm to obtain a monthly time series of firm-level IV level and skew. The average firm has an IV level of 24.7%and skew of 4.0%⁠.

Equity market data come from CRSP, and data on corporate policies from the quarterly Compustat files. Debt is measured as debt in current liabilities (DLCQ) plus long-term debt (DLTTQ). Using CRSP data, market equity is the product of share price (PRC) and the number of shares outstanding (SHROUT). We define leverage as debt divided by debt plus market equity. The average firm in our sample has a leverage ratio of 18.7%⁠.

To mitigate the effect of outliers, we winsorize all firm-level variables at the 0.5th and 99.5th percentiles within each calendar year.Table 5reports the mean and standard deviation of quasi-market leverage, excess returns, the 5-year CDS rate, at-the-money IV, and the IV skew. All moments are pooled across firms and time.

Table 2summarizes predefined parameters. We followBansal and Yaron (2004)in assuming a value ofψ=1.5for the representative agent’s EIS, an assumption that results in a smooth risk-free rate. The time discount rate,β=0.9981⁠, is chosen so that the average 1-year risk-free rate equals 1%in normal times.10Different fromBansal and Yaron (2004), our model features a crisis state, which necessitates the estimation of risk aversion. The correlation between consumption and earnings is set to 20%⁠, as estimated byBhamra, Kuehn, and Strebulaev (2010).

While in reality equity investors receive payoffs in the form of both cash dividends and capital gains, our model abstracts from retained earnings. It is therefore appropriate to calibrate the tax rate on cash dividends in the model based on the tax rate for total equity income in the data. We followGraham (2000)in computing the tax rate on equity income asτe=(d+(1−d)δ)τd⁠, wheredequals the payout ratio,τdthe tax rate on dividend income, andδmeasures the benefit of deferring capital gains. For our sample period, the average tax rate on dividend income equals 17.2%⁠, while the average payout ratio of firms in our sample equals 45.6%⁠. Using the same estimate ofδ=0.25as inGraham (2000), we findτe=11.2%⁠.11We also followGraham (2000)in assuming a tax rate on interest income ofτi=29.6%⁠. The corporate tax rate is based onGraham(1996a,1996b). Using Graham’s data for tax rates of individual firms and years, we compute the pooled average marginal tax rate (before interest expenses) for our sample of firms and sample period asτc=32.9%⁠.

Finally, we have to calibrate debt and equity issuance costs. We set the debt issuance costs at 1%and equity issuance costs at 4%based on the evidence inAltinkilic and Hansen (2000)for large firms.

2.4 Simulated method of moments

The remaining model parameters are estimated with the simulated method of moments (SMM). Given the predefined parameters and vectorθ=(ϕμ,ϕσ,0,ϕσ,1,ϕζ,0,ϕζ,1,ω0,ω1,γ)⁠, we solve the model numerically and simulate a panel of firms. AppendixEdiscusses details of the numerical solution method. The SMM objective function equals a weighted distance metric between model moments from the simulated panelΨM(θ)and moments from actual dataΨD⁠,J(θ)=[ΨD−ΨM(θ)]′W[ΨD−ΨM(θ)]⁠. The weighting matrixWequals the inverse of the spectral density, which is computed via influence functions as inHennessy and Whited (2007). The parameter estimateθ^is found by searching globally over the parameter space, which we implement via a particle swarm algorithm. Because the numerical model solution is computationally expensive, the estimation requires a high-performance computing environment. Computing standard errors for the parameter estimate requires the Jacobian of the moment vector, which we find numerically via a finite difference method. AppendixFdetails the calculation of standard errors.

A noteworthy feature of our 2004–2019 sample is the occurrence of the Great Recession, an episode that led to a−2.14%drop in aggregate consumption between Q3-2008 to Q4-2009. This value is small relative to the consumption drop in a typical macroeconomic crisis, but also large relative to what is typically observed in noncrisis states. For estimating the model, it would therefore not be appropriate to simulate model data from the ergodic distribution of the aggregate state, and it would be similarly inappropriate to rule out crises. Instead, we base the estimation on simulated data that mimics the amount of macroeconomic risk in our sample. Specifically, we simulate 5,000 monthly panels of 100 firms over 186 months (the size of the actual data sample) and ensure that the worst consumption drop in each panel equals−2.14%±0.1%⁠, as in the data. We find that 27.8%of such paths contain the occurrence of state 3. From the perspective of our model, it is therefore possible, but not extremely likely, that the Great Recession reflected a short-lived disaster episode.

In simulating the firm panels, we replace defaulting firms with new ones, initialized at optimal (state-dependent) leverage. Since the market value of debt is not observable for public firms, we compute leverage based on book value of debt, which is typically referred to as quasi-market leverage. We mimic quasi-market leverage in the model by relying on the value of debt from the most recent issuance date.

2.5 Identification

The eight estimated parameters(ϕμ,ϕσ,0,ϕσ,1,ϕζ,0,ϕζ,1,ω0,ω1,γ)are identified based on eleven moments. At the firm-level, we include the mean and variance of quasi-market leverage, excess returns, the 5-year CDS rate, at-the-money IV, and the IV skew. As in the data, these moments are pooled across firms and time. At the aggregate level, we include the variance of market returns. Ideally, one would additionally include moments of earnings growth rates as an empirical target for the model. Unfortunately, however, firm-level earnings(a)cannot be observed monthly,(b)are subject to large measurement noise because of seasonalities and “earnings manipulation,” and(c)can become negative, so that growth rates are not well-defined.12Each parameter affects multiple moments; still, it is useful to discuss the main sources of identification.Table 3reports the sensitivity of momentiwith respect to parameterj⁠,dΨiMdθjθjΨiM⁠, evaluated at the point estimates fromTable 4. We compute the derivativedΨiMdθjbased on central finite differences (see AppendixFfor details).

The information content of both option and credit moments is crucial for the identification of time-varying bankruptcy costs. While CDS rates are sensitive to both default probabilities and expected loss rates to bond holders, equity options are only sensitive to the former because stockholders recover (close to) nothing in default. Based on both instruments, we are therefore able to separately identify time variation in bankruptcy costs (which affects only CDS) and time-varying cash flow risks (which affects both options and CDS).

The level of bankruptcy costsω0is mainly identified by average leverage because firms optimally reduce leverage as bankruptcy costs rise. In addition, lower leverage decreases default risk and thus credit spreads. Importantly, credit spreads are convex in leverage. Thus, CDS rates provide an additional source of identification for bankruptcy costs.

Compared to the level of bankruptcy costs, their cyclicalityω1has a larger impact on second moments. Firms optimally reduce not only the level but also the dispersion in leverage when bankruptcy costs become more countercyclical. Hence, the cyclicality is identified by the variance of leverage. Because a reduction in leverage dispersion reduces the variance of CDS rates, at-the-money IV and IV skew, these asset pricing moments are also informative. Equity options are sensitive to leverage because they represent compound options.

Risk aversionγincreases all risk premiums, and is therefore identified by average excess returns, CDS rates, and option skew. As in the long-run risk framework, the representative investor’s recursive utility function implies aversion against persistent variation in conditional growth rates. An increase in the drift scaling parameterϕμincreases risk premiums and is therefore identified by average excess returns, CDS rates, and IV skew. In addition, an increase in the drift scaling parameter leads to more dispersion in earnings growth, which raises the variance of CDS rates, at-the-money IV and IV skew.

The level of idiosyncraticϕζ,0and aggregate riskϕσ,0are positively related to the volatility of firm-level returns and average at-the-money IV. Because idiosyncratic shocks average out in the aggregate, however, the aggregate volatility scaling parameterϕσ,0is identified by the volatility of the market return.

The cyclicality of idiosyncraticϕζ,1and aggregate riskϕσ,1have a large impact on the time variation of CDS rates. When risk becomes more countercyclical, firms optimally reduce leverage. This effect dominates the direct risk channel so that credit spreads become less volatile as the cyclicality of idiosyncratic and aggregate risk increases. Yet, relative to the cyclicality of idiosyncratic risk, the cyclicality of aggregate risk has a more significant effect on time variation in the IV skew, which therefore helps to separately identifyϕζ,1andϕσ,1⁠.

The benchmark estimation matches the target moments well. In the model, quasi-market leverage averages 18.4%(data: 18.7%⁠) with a standard deviation of 12.5%(data: 14.4%⁠). To match these moments, the estimation sets the level of bankruptcy costs toω0=0.203and their cyclicality toω1=0.102⁠. While true market leverage is not observable in the data, our model implies that it has an average of 17.0%and a standard deviation of 9.8%⁠. Interestingly, average quasi-market leverage is therefore an upward-biased proxy of average true leverage. The reason being that leverage is a concave function of the bond price, so that more bond price variability translates to higher average leverage because of the Jensen effect. Similarly, because stock and bond returns are positively correlated, leverage ratios based on market values are less volatile. To our knowledge, this finding is new and appears to be useful for interpreting the findings of empirical work on firms’ capital structure.

In the model, average firm-level excess returns equal 0.65%(data: 0.80%⁠) per month. Risk premiums in our model are generated by persistent drift and volatility differences across states, as in the long-run risks literature.13In fact, the estimated magnitude of the drift scaling parameterϕμ=3.8is close to the calibrated value of three inBansal and Yaron (2004). The volatility of excess returns equals 7.2%(data: 6.9%⁠) at the individual firm level and 5.0%(data: 5.3%⁠) for the market, both close to their data counterparts. To replicate the ratio of idiosyncratic to aggregate risk, the estimation selects average idiosyncratic risk ofϕζ,0=0.044and a smaller average aggregate risk ofϕσ,0=0.032⁠.

Related to stock return volatility are option moments. The model closely matches the average firm-level at-the-money IV of 24.5%(data: 24.7%⁠) with a standard deviation of 9.8%(data: 10.7%⁠). The IV skew, which equals the difference between the one-standard-deviation out-of-the money IV and at-the-money IV, averages 3.6%(data: 4.0%⁠) with a standard deviation of 1.1%(data: 2.0%⁠). Lastly, the model generates an average 5-year CDS rate at 59 bps (data: 61 bps) with a standard deviation of 0.84%(data: 0.97%⁠).14The volatility scaling parameters for idiosyncraticϕζ,1=10.7and aggregate riskϕσ,1=25.0are crucial for the variability of the IV curve and CDS rates. Our estimation thus confirms that common idiosyncratic volatility in earnings growth is a robust feature of the data, as recently documented byHerskovic et al. (2016).

We estimate a value of 7.9 for the representative agent’s coefficient of relative risk aversionγ⁠. This value falls between the one inBarro (2006), who assumes a value of 3 to 4, andBansal and Yaron (2004), whose main specification assumes a value of 10. Our estimate is intuitive, because the long-lasting nature of crises makes them less painful in utility terms than the instantaneous disasters inBarro (2006), all else equal. Hence, higher risk aversion is required to generate realistic risk premiums. Similarly, we require less risk aversion thanBansal and Yaron (2004)because our consumption process implies somewhat larger shocks than those in the long-run risks specification. Even though the economic fit of the model is very good, statistically the model is rejected with aJ-statistic of 19.7.

InTable 5, the column Population refers to a simulation based on the unconditional distribution of the aggregate state, that is, including severe consumption disasters. With more severe crises, risk premiums increase. Average excess stock returns increase to 0.67%⁠, the 5-year CDS rate to 92 bps, at-the-money IV to 27.1%⁠, and the IV skew to 3.7%⁠. The patterns all match the historical U.S. experience, where average returns, return volatilities, and credit spreads were all higher in data going back to the 1930’s than in the postwar sample.

2.7 Disasters or long-run risks?

The column LRR inTables 4and5refers to an estimation where we calibrate consumption as in the long-run risks literature, but leave assumptions about preferences and firms unchanged. The resultant model resembles those ofBhamra, Kuehn, and Strebulaev (2010)andChen (2010). The Markov-switching process for consumption approximates the long-run risks process ofBansal and Yaron (2004), and is calibrated as inBonomo et al. (2010). We calibrate risk aversion to a value of 10.15The purpose of this exercise is to illustrate the effect of the crisis state on corporate policies and asset prices.

For our data set, the long-run risks estimation fails to explain risk premiums: the average excess return, 5-year CDS rates, at-the-money IV, and IV skew are all much lower than in the data. In contrast,Bhamra, Kuehn, and Strebulaev (2010)andChen (2010)find that the long-run risks model can explain the credit spread puzzle. The difference arises from the fact that both prior papers do not use a common set of firms to calibrate leverage and credit spreads, whereas our structural estimation relies on a common data set for firms in the S&P 100 index. Among these firms, the credit spread puzzle is particularly pronounced with leverage ratios below 20%and CDS rates around 60 bps. In contrast, bothBhamra, Kuehn, and Strebulaev (2010)andChen (2010)calibrate to leverage ratios of nearly 40%⁠.

2.8 Time-series versus cross-sectional dispersion

For the SMM estimation, we pool observations across firms and months to compute the variance of firm-level variables. These variances therefore reflect both time-series and cross-sectional sources of variation. Intuitively, cross-sectional dispersion primarily reflects idiosyncratic shocks, whereas time-series variation additionally reflects aggregate shocks. In this section, we assess whether the model is consistent with the relative importance of these two separate sources of variation. To measure cross-sectional dispersion, we compute the variance (across firms) of the variables in each period, and then average the estimates across all months in the sample. To measure time-series variation, we compute the variance of the variables (across time) for each firm, and then average the estimates across firms.

Table 6shows the square root of both variance measures and compares them to the pooled dispersion measure in the SMM estimation. We see that cross-sectional dispersion in leverage and CDS rates is nearly as high as pooled dispersion, but quite a bit lower in the time-series. For returns and the two IV measures, the pattern is reversed: time-series dispersion is as high or nearly as high as pooled dispersion, but quite a bit lower than cross-sectional dispersion.16The model replicates these patterns qualitatively and provides a reasonably good fit quantitatively. Therefore, the model appears to realistically depict the relative importance of systematic and idiosyncratic risks.

article.3. Macroeconomic Crises:
This section examines the model’s ability to replicate the dynamics of financial leverage and asset market risks during macroeconomic crises, such as the Great Depression. Because these features were not targeted by the estimation, they serve as a useful out-of-sample test of the model’s mechanism. For consistency with the estimation, we focus on Great Depression data of the largest firms, the creditworthiness of which likely differs from that of the average firm.

It is worth noting that many institutional details that affect firms’ incentives to issue debt differed between the 1929–1933 Great Depression period and our 2004–2019 estimation sample. Coincidentally, however, it appears that the net benefit of debt was comparable across the two periods, as firms’ leverage ratios before the Great Depression and during our estimation sample were nearly identical at about 20%(seeFigure 1;Table 5). Our model therefore provides a realistic depiction of the net benefits of debt during the Great Depression, even though the relative importance of tax rates, issuance costs, bankruptcy costs, and other factors may have varied over time. It therefore seems appropriate to rely on the model’s ability to capture the quantitative relationship between leverage and asset market risks during the Great Depression as a means to evaluate its economic mechanism.

3.1 Great Depression data

We take monthly data on the market value of firms’ equity from CRSP, and hand-collect annual data on firms’ book value of debt over the years from 1927 to 1935 from Moody’s Industrial Manuals. We include all sources of long-term debt, common examples of which are funded debt, bonded debt, notes and loans payable, as well as bonds of subsidiaries.17Starting with the 100 largest nonfinancial, nonutility companies with available book debt and market equity data, we eliminate firms with zero leverage (mimicking the elimination of firms without CDS quotes). Leverage is defined as book debt, divided by the sum of book debt and market equity.

As in the model estimation, we measure equity and default risk based on option and CDS contracts. Because neither derivative was traded at the time of the Great Depression, we compute the corresponding measures based on a projection. Specifically, using data from January 2004 to June 2019 (the sample used in the SMM estimation), we regress the time series of average at-the-money IVs and CDS rates on (1) average leverage among the set of firms in our sample, (2) the realized volatility of daily returns on the CRSP value-weighted stock market index over the past calendar month, and (3) the Moody’s Baa-Aaa corporate bond yield spread from FRED. The regressions result inR2’s of 89.5%for implied volatility and 82.9%for CDS rates. Based on these estimated regressions, we infer implied average IVs and CDS rates for the Great Depression using data for the three explanatory variables over the period from August 1928 to August 1936.

Figure 1in the introduction shows the resultant time series in event time. According to the NBER, the Great Depression began in August of 1929. Because consumption data for this period are only available at an annual frequency, we sett=0at the beginning of 1930 to ensure that consumption and firm-level data are temporally aligned. The figure shows that consumption losses, financial leverage, equity volatility, and credit spreads all rise in tandem, peak during 1932, and then gradually return to their precrisis levels.18Figure 3shows an analogous pattern for stock prices and default rates. In particular, stock prices continue to fall while the crisis endures because the deterioration of corporate earnings increases default risk. The strong co-movement between the series confirms the intuition that financial leverage is an important determinant of conditional asset market risks. Specifically, falling stock prices increase leverage and make stock returns more volatile going forward, thereby increasing the probability of default and credit spreads on corporate bonds. We return to the relation between default risk and option prices in Section4.4, where we examine it cross-sectionally.

3.2 Simulated Great Depression

The Great Depression is mimicked in the model based on simulations that replicate the observed drop in aggregate consumption. Specifically, we simulate the monthly consumption process based on its unconditional dynamics, time-aggregate it to an annual frequency, and then search for episodes during which consecutive negative annual growth rates accumulate to−16.58%±0.25%⁠, as in the period from 1930 to 1933.19Next, we evaluate leverage policies and asset prices for 100 firms and average them in each period. The dotted lines inFigures 1and3represent the mean of these firm-level averages across 100,000 simulated crises. In all plots,t=0marks the beginning of the first year with a negative consumption growth rate. It is worth noting that, since we are dating disasters based on annual consumption data, simulations differ in terms of the exact month in which the crisis begins. When we average across many simulations, jumps in asset prices due to the switch to the crisis state are therefore smoothed across the months surroundingt=0⁠.

The model does a nice job of capturing key features of the crisis. Consumption losses extend over multiple years. Leverage, equity volatility, credit spreads, and default rates all rise in tandem while the crisis endures and gradually return to precrisis levels over subsequent years. For stock prices, the pattern is mirrored on the downside.20The model slightly undershoots the observed swings in stock prices and leverage, but replicates peak equity volatility and credit spreads nearly perfectly. It is worth emphasizing that, while we calibrate the consumption process to data from the Great Depression, the behavior of leverage and asset prices during prolonged crises does not serve as an input to the estimation. The model’s ability to match these dynamics can therefore be viewed as an external validation of its mechanism.

Through the lens of the model, we can assess the relative importance of different drivers of CDS rates during crises. In particular, CDS rates reflect default probabilities, expected losses to bondholders in the case of default, and risk premiums.Figure 4shows each of these objects in an event window plot. We see that cumulative 5-year default probabilities rise from near zero before the crisis to about 2%per year in the third year of the crisis, whereas 5-year expected loss rates increase only slightly. Risk premiums on default probabilities, that is, differences in default probabilities under the physical and risk-neutral measure, nearly quadruple over the same period, from 0.56%to 1.94%per year. In contrast, risk premiums on loss rates remain small. The model therefore suggests that time variation in default probabilities and the associated risk premium are the main drivers of CDS rates during crises.

Figure 4 Determinants of CDS Rates during Macroeconomic Crises We plot conditional 5-year default probabilities (left panel) and expected 5-year loss rates in the case of default (middle panel), under both the true and risk-neutral probability measures, as well as realized loss rates and bankruptcy costs (right panel). All moments are based on the same crisis simulation as Figure 1 . Open in new tab Download slide

Interestingly, realized loss rates vary substantially over the course of the crisis and are predominantly driven by time variation in bankruptcy costs (shown in the bottom panel ofFigure 4). Since the persistence of crises is fairly low, however, expected loss rates over a 5-year window do not increase nearly as much as realized losses when the economy enters the crises regime. As a result, expected losses are not an important driver of time variation in 5-year CDS during crises.

Figure 5illustrates how leverage affects the riskiness of equity during crises. We plot at-the-money implied volatilities for equity options, as well as options that are written on firms’ unlevered assets. There are two effects. First, the onset of a crisis is characterized by a drop in stock prices and increase in leverage, which causes equity volatility to increase more significantly than asset volatility. Second, leverage continues to rise while the crisis endures, which increases default risk and thereby equity volatility, without a corresponding change in asset volatility. To understand time variation in equity volatility, it is therefore important to allow for time-varying leverage, which the typicalAbel (1999)approach does not capture.

To put the model’s implications into perspective, it is useful to compare them to those of standard disaster risk models.Wachter (2013)explains time variation in aggregate stock market volatility via time variation in the probability of rare disasters. In her model, an increase in the disaster probability lowers cash flow expectations and increases discount rates, which in turn lowers the market’s price-dividend ratio. This mechanism is consistent with the observed excess volatility of returns relative to cash flows. Relatedly,Seo and Wachter (2018)rely on a similar mechanism to explain time variation in the CDX index, a well-known gauge of credit spreads among large firms. In their model, an increase in the disaster probability lowers firms’ asset values and leads to a default if the asset value drops below an exogenously specified boundary. Periods of elevated disaster risk are therefore associated with increased credit spreads. Because the model abstracts from firms’ capital structure decisions, however, it cannot account for the effect of falling equity values on financial leverage and the resultant increases in conditional debt and equity risk.

Both models describe disasters as instantaneous IID events, and we therefore mimic the Great Depression by imposing that a consumption disaster of−16.58%±1%occurs att=0⁠. Because disasters are more likely to occur when the conditional disaster probability is high, both the conditional equity volatility (in the Wachter model) and credit spreads (in the Seo-Wachter model) are elevated during disasters. As the dashed lines inFigure 1represent, however, this effect is quantitatively negligible in both cases. The reason being that disaster events neither (a) alter the conditional distribution of firms’ cash flows because they are IID nor (b) increase firms’ leverage ratios. In contrast, our empirical results suggests that the interaction of crisis persistence and financial leverage is important for understanding conditional asset price dynamics.

3.3 Crisis severity

A distinguishing feature of our model is that more severe crises lead to larger spikes in asset market risks. In particular, worse crises tend to extend over longer periods, so that consumption and earnings losses slowly accumulate over time. The erosion of corporate earnings makes stocks less valuable, which increases financial leverage and measures of risk in equity and debt markets.Figure 6illustrates this channel quantitatively. The figure plots leverage, equity volatility, and credit spreads as a function of cumulative consumption losses during the crisis.

Figure 6 Crisis severity and asset prices This figure illustrates the link between crisis severity, as measured by the cumulative drop in consumption, and the peak in leverage, equity volatility, and credit spreads during the crisis. Open in new tab Download slide

The figure shows that, while mild crises lead to only slight increases in financial leverage in the model, they nevertheless lead to large spikes in equity volatility and credit spreads. Intuitively, this feature reflects uncertainty about the crisis duration and the associated uncertainty about firms’ future cash flows, and it is consistent with the evidence ofKrishnamurthy and Muir (2020)as well as the U.S. experience during the Great Recession of 2008–2009. Using an international panel data set, Krishnamurthy and Muir document that the onset of a crisis typically coincides with large spikes in credit spreads. Similarly, the Great Recession resulted in consumption losses of only 2.2%over 18 months and average leverage ratios (for the largest 100 firms) that remained under 30%⁠, but saw equity volatility spike to nearly 70%and credit spreads rise to 3.3%⁠.

In contrast, the canonical rare disaster model abstracts from feedback of macroeconomic contractions on asset markets. More severe macroeconomic contractions are still associated with larger declines in asset values, but their magnitude has no effect on equity volatility and credit spreads because the model abstracts from financial leverage.
article.4. Determinants of Credit Spreads and Op:
This section explores the economic mechanism behind the model’s ability to match firm-level CDS and option prices. We discuss our estimates of bankruptcy costs and explain how the model resolves the credit spread puzzle. Next, we highlight the importance of leverage for option prices and illustrate the relation between default risk and option prices.

4.1 Bankruptcy costs

Table 7reports credit market moments that were not targeted by the estimation. In line with existing literature, we find that average bankruptcy costs are sizable on average at 20%with a standard deviation of 10%⁠. For instance,Andrade and Kaplan (1998)estimate distress costs of 10%–23%of firm value for a sample of 31 highly leveraged transactions,Hennessy and Whited (2007)find that bankruptcy costs equal to 8.4%of capital (not asset value) for large and 15.1%for small firms,Davydenko, Strebulaev, and Zhao (2012)estimate an average default cost of 21.7%of the market value of assets from a sample of 175 defaulted firms, andGlover (2016)structurally estimates average cost of 25%among defaulted firms.

While bankruptcy costs capture a variety of factors, such as reputation costs, asset fire sales, loss of customer or supplier relationships, legal and accounting fees, and costs of changing management, loss rates on bonds additionally reflect reduced asset values that result from a history of adverse shocks. The corporate finance literature, includingJankowitsch, Nagler, and Subrahmanyam (2014)andNozawa (2017), has provided evidence in favor of countercyclical loss rates on bonds. We find that bankruptcy costs are strongly countercyclical as well. This finding is new to the literature. Gauging the time-varying nature of bankruptcy costs requires a structural model because empirical loss rates can be time varying due to procyclical asset values. Specifically, firms tend to default in recessions when their asset values are depressed, thus leading to larger losses for bond investors even when bankruptcy costs are constant.

Because the representative investor is risk averse, cyclicality drives a wedge between expected bankruptcy costs under the physical and risk-neutral measure. In particular, average risk-neutral bankruptcy costs are 36.6%⁠, compared to 20.3%under the physical measure.21This evidence suggests that structural estimations with risk-neutral agents, as is common in corporate finance models, may result in biased estimates. In contrast, our framework implies that observable bankruptcy costs are much smaller, while the model is still consistent with low leverage. Our exercise therefore highlights the importance of risk adjustments in structural corporate finance models.

Similar toChen (2010), cyclical bankruptcy costs help to explain the low leverage puzzle. When we reestimate the model with constant bankruptcy costs, we find that the model requires a much higher value of bankruptcy costs of 37.9%to generate low leverage in our data. Intuitively, firms are reluctant to issue debt when bankruptcy costs are high on average or when they are high in states of high default risk with high marginal utility. Interestingly, the fixed bankruptcy cost estimate is close to the average risk-neutral one, where we allow for time variation in bankruptcy costs.

4.2 Credit spread puzzle

A solution to the credit spread puzzle requires that firms have low leverage and low physical default probabilities. As shown inTable 5, the model generates low leverage of around 18%⁠, as in the data. Similarly, the model implies a low 5-year default probability of only 23 bps. Empirically, we observe a single default event in our sample (General Motors in 2009), which equates to a 5-year default probability of 52 bps.22Obviously, this is a very noisy estimate because of the small sample size.

While most papers on the credit spread puzzle focus on the Moody’s sample starting in 1970 to measure average default rates,Bhamra, Kuehn, and Strebulaev (2010)andFeldhütter and Schaefer (2018)stress the importance of using default data going back to 1920. Since default events are rare, using only 47 years of data can introduce large sampling error in the observed average rate. Over 1920–2017, the average 5-year default probability for investment-grade firms equaled 1.5%(Ou et al. (2018), exhibit 32 ). The “Population” column inTable 7shows credit market moments from the unconditional distribution of the aggregate Markov chain in our model, which includes severe macroeconomic crises, such as the Great Depression. In the long sample, severe crises lead to waves in default. As a result, the average 5-year default probability rises more than threefold to 77 bps. The consumption and earnings processes are therefore equipped to generate both postwar and long-sample dynamics that are close to the data. This is a contribution relative to the existing credit risk literature, which has focused on either postwar or long sample dynamics.

Even though actual default probabilities are low, CDS rates are sizable on average due to a risk compensation for cyclical default risk. Specifically, the conditional risk-neutral default probability can be decomposed into the actual default probability and a risk adjustment, measured by a covariance with the pricing kernel. Since defaults tend to cluster in crises when marginal utility is high, the covariance is positive and significant. In the model, the average risk-neutral 5-year default probability is more than 10 times larger than the average physical one, at about 318 bps. At the same time, risk-neutral default probabilities are more volatile than physical ones.

Given our bankruptcy costs estimates, the model generates average 1-month LGD of 70%under the physical measure, which is slightly higher than in the data.Ou et al.(2018, exhibit 8) report an average senior unsecured bond loss rates of 52.1%for the years 1987 to 2017. Because the model abstracts from indirect equity issuance costs, such as costs associated with informational frictions, it is relatively cheap for equity holders to keep the firm solvent, even in bad times. As a result, shareholders declare default at very low asset values, inflating average LGD.

4.3 Option prices

Because options are contingent claims on firms’ equity and priced by imposing the absence of arbitrage, a model’s ability to match option prices is closely linked to its ability to provide a realistic account of stock market risk premiums. This section therefore explores the mechanism behind our model’s ability to capture option prices in more detail.

We follow the common approach of expressing option prices in Black-Scholes-Merton implied volatility units.Figure 7shows IVs for the 1-month maturity as a function of standardized moneyness, defined asK/SσATM/12⁠. The dashed black line represents the data, and the solid red line represents the model. The remaining lines will be discussed below. We average IVs across firms and months, relying on the same simulation as for the SMM estimation.

The level of the IV curve is determined by the risk-neutral volatility of stock returns, which reflects cash flow volatility, volatility due to financial leverage, and a risk premium for variance risk. We discuss these channels in reverse order. The variance premium for the aggregate stock market is commonly measured by the difference between the squared VIX index and an estimate of the conditional return variance under the physical measure.Martin (2017)shows that the squared VIX measures the risk-neutral entropy of market returns,VIX2=log⁡EQ[R]−EQ[log⁡(R)]⁠. Being defined in terms of the log function, an equivalent to the VIX at the firm level does not exist, because the possibility of default implies a nonzero probability for returns of−100%⁠. We therefore quantify the variance premium by the difference between the average at-the-money IV and the annualized volatility of monthly returns instead. While this measure of the variance premium is large for the aggregate stock market,23Table 5shows that the firm-level premium is negligible: the average at-the-money IV equals 24.7%and return volatility12×6.93%=24.0%⁠. Our model replicates this fact, as it matches both the volatility of firms’ returns and the average at-the-money IV well. To quantify the amount of volatility due to financial leverage, we also compute an average IV curve for options that are written on firms’ unlevered assets. A comparison between the solid red and dotted blue lines inFigure 7shows that the at-the-money asset volatility is on average 4.8 percentage points lower than the at-the-money equity volatility. Despite its low average, financial leverage therefore has a sizable effect on the volatility of equity returns. We view this gap as a realistic estimate because our model matches the level and variability of firms’ leverage ratios. The remaining 19.7%(in absolute terms) of firms’ at-the-money IV reflect asset volatility.

The slope of the IV curve, that is, the IV skew, reflects the negative risk-neutral skewness of stock returns. While cash flow shocks in our model are conditionally Gaussian, the possibility of macroeconomic crises and firm defaults implies that stock returns are not. In particular, crises are associated with low cash flow growth rates and high volatility, so that firms’ valuation ratios drop when the economy enters a crisis. In normal times, the potential of a crisis therefore induces negative skewness. Similarly, defaults induce negative skewness because they are associated with returns of−100%⁠. The fact that our model replicates the shape of the IV curve, as the solid red line inFigure 7represents, suggests that it produces a realistic amount of risk-neutral skewness in returns. To gauge the relative importance of each source of skewness, we again turn to the IV curve for options on firms’ unlevered assets.24Obviously, default risk plays no role for the pricing of asset options. The dotted blue line inFigure 7represents that the asset option skew equals 3.36, which is 7%lower than the corresponding equity option skew. Macroeconomic crises, rather than default risk due to financial leverage, are therefore the main source of skewness in the model. Despite its moderate effect on firms’averagerisk-neutral skewness, we show in Section4.4that default risk results in sizablecross-sectional differencesin risk-neutral skewness.

Prior work on disaster risk has focused on the pricing of index, rather than individual firm options. WhileBackus, Chernov, and Martin (2011)show that the index IV curve in the constant disaster risk model ofBarro (2006)is considerably steeper than in the data,Gabaix (2012)andSeo and Wachter (2019)point out that this is a result of unrealistically low return volatility in normal times. Models with time-varying disaster risk, such as those ofGabaix (2012)andWachter (2013), which produce realistic amounts of stock return volatility, also produce index IV curves that provide a good match for their empirical counterpart. Because our model provides a similarly good match for the volatility of returns in normal times, its ability to capture the IV skew largely reflects the same channel highlighted in these prior papers. To illustrate this explicitly, we set idiosyncratic risk to zero,ζt=0⁠, and compute the price of options written on a claim to unlevered aggregate earnings. This claim can be interpreted as the analogue to aggregate equity in the aforementioned papers, as it reflectsAbel (1999)-type leverage rather than true financial leverage. The dash-dotted blue line inFigure 7shows that the slope of the IV curve for aggregate asset options increases to 4.31. Intuitively, the aggregate asset IV curve is steeper than the firm-level asset IV curve because systematic tail risk carries a risk premium, while idiosyncratic tail risk does not. Nevertheless, the aggregate IV curve in our model remains substantially flatter than its equivalent in theBarro (2006)model, similar to its equivalent in the models ofGabaix (2012)andWachter (2013).

4.4 Default risk and option prices

To motivate our estimation approach, we argue that option prices contain valuable information about cash flow risks and default probabilities. A natural question is therefore whether firms’ IV curves vary systematically with default risk in the data. To test this implication, we sort firms into deciles based on Merton’s distance-to-default (DD) measure, a proxy for default risk that can be easily computed in the model and data. DD is defined asln⁡[(E+D)/D]+(ri−0.5σV2)TσVT⁠, whereEis the value of equity,Dthe value of debt,rithe firm’s stock return over the past year,Tthe horizon in years, andσVthe firm’s asset volatility. We followBharath and Shumway (2008)and implement their so-called naïve DD measure.25They find that the naïve DD performs better than the original Merton’s DD measure in predicting defaults out-of-sample, while being based on the same inputs and easier to compute. Because we are interested in whether short-term option contracts are informative about the longer-horizon default probabilities reflected in CDS, we set the DD horizon toT=5years.

Within each DD decile, we compute the average at-the-money IV, IV skew, and CDS rate across firms.Table 8shows that, in the model, at-the-money IV decreases monotonically from 29.7%(data: 34.7%⁠) for firms with the highest default risk to 23.2%(data: 20.0%⁠) for firms with the lowest default risk. This pattern is intuitive, because higher default risk reflects a combination of higher asset volatility and higher leverage, both of which are associated with higher stock return volatility. We also find that the IV skew decreases from 3.9%(data: 4.4%⁠) to 3.5%(data: 3.7%⁠) across deciles. The reason being that default events result in equity returns of−100%⁠, so that higher default probabilities are associated with more negative conditional return skewness. Clearly, the model does a nice job of capturing the relation between default risk and option prices, despite the fact that these moments were not targeted by the estimation. While perhaps qualitatively unsurprising, the model also provides a good match for the quantitative relationship between DD and CDS rates, which drop from 1.7%(data: 2.3%⁠) for firms with the highest default risk to 0.3%(data: 0.3%⁠) for firms with the lowest default risk. These findings suggest that the model provides a realistic mapping between the volatility of firms’ cash flows, their endogenous capital structure decisions, and the conditional distribution of their stock returns.

article.5. Conclusion:
Macroeconomic crises provide a unique window into connections between asset markets and the real economy. In this paper, we propose an asset pricing model that matches the joint dynamics of aggregate consumption, firms’ leverage ratios, and asset prices during macroeconomic crises. In particular, the model replicates the fact that longer-lasting crises are associated with bigger macroeconomic contractions, larger increases in financial leverage, credit spreads, and equity volatility, and significantly more firm defaults. Capturing these dynamics is important because the nature of potential crises is a central driver of asset prices even during normal times.

Long-lasting crises enable the model to explain risk premiums across equity, credit, and option markets. Specifically, we show that leverage explains a significant part of the time-series and cross-sectional variation in equity option prices. Because options reflect equity risks, default risk should also not be ignored when modeling stock prices. Yet, state-of-the-art asset pricing models ignore time variation in leverage. As a result, they abstract from an important source of time variation in asset market risks.

Lastly, a structural estimation of our model reveals a large gap between historical and risk-neutral bankruptcy costs. This finding highlights the importance of risk-adjustments in structural corporate models, where investors have traditionally been modeled as risk neutral. Overall, we believe that our work provides a step toward a joint framework for macroeconomics, asset pricing, and corporate finance.

C. CDS Valuation

Firm i defaults at time t i when its interest coverage ratio κ i , t drops below the default threshold κ t D ⁠ , so that t i ≡ inf { t : κ i , t ≤ κ t D } .

The CDS rate z i , t , s T for horizon T equates the present value of the payments made by the insurance seller and the insurance buyer. The insurance seller receives the CDS rate until the expiration date T or firm default. In the case of default, bond holders take over the firm and recover the after-tax firm value net of bankruptcy costs, ( 1 − ω t + h ) A i , t + h ⁠ , such that their losses relative to the initial investment, D i , s ⁠ , are given by x i , t + h , s = 1 − ( 1 − ω t + h ) A i , t + h D i , s .

By no-arbitrage, the one-period CDS rate z i , t , s 1 is given by z 1 ( κ i , t , ξ s , ξ t ) = E t Q [ 1 { t i = t + 1 } x i , t + 1 , s ] 1 − E t Q [ 1 { t i = t + 1 } ] = H i , t , s 1 1 − q i , t 1 , where H i , t , s 1 is the risk-neutral one-period expected loss rate and q i , t 1 the risk-neutral one-period conditional default probability.
