D. Option Valuation

The value of a one period put option with strike price K is given by P i , t = E t [ M t , t + 1 max { K − 1 { κ t + 1 I ≤ κ i , t + 1 } Δ i , t + 1 − S i , t + 1 x , 0 } ] , where S i , t x denotes the ex-dividend share price and the term 1 { κ t + 1 I ≤ κ i , t + 1 } Δ i , t + 1 reflects an adjustment for special dividends. Because special dividends and other corporate actions (stock splits, mergers, etc.) lead to discrete jumps in the underlying’s price, the contractual terms of equity options are altered when such events occur. In the case of a special dividend, the strike price is lowered by the amount of the dividend.

Expressed in stationary units, the put price-earnings ratio λ i , t p = P i , t / E i , t equals λ i , t p = e − r t f E t Q [ { λ i , t s , x X − 1 { κ t + 1 I ≤ κ i , t + 1 } e g i , t + 1 Δ ~ i , t + 1 − e g i , t + 1 λ i , t + 1 s , x } + ] , where X = K / S i , t x is the option’s moneyness and λ i , t s , x is the ex-dividend price-earnings ratio. Because the Black-Scholes-Merton formula is homogeneous of degree zero in the spot price of the underlying asset and the strike price, implied volatilities can then be found based on λ i , t p and X from λ i , t B S = N ( − d 2 ) e − r t X ( λ i , t s , x − λ i , t d i v ) − N ( − d 1 ) ( λ i , t s , x − λ i , t d i v ) , where λ i , t d i v is the ratio of ordinary dividends-to-earnings and d 1 = 1 σ ( ln ⁡ 1 X + ( r + σ 2 / 2 ) ) d 2 = d 1 − σ .

Note that the mapping from option prices to Black-Scholes-Merton implied volatilities (IV) relies on the state-dependent risk-free rate and dividend yield.

E. Numerical Solution

We use a standard nonlinear equation solver to find the state-dependent wealth-consumption ratioλc(ξt)from (3). Based on the pricing kernel (2), we can then compute the 1-period risk-free rate asRtf=1/Et[Mt+1]analytically. The asset-to-earnings ratioλa(ξt)of an unlevered firm can be computed based on (B.1). We use Gaussian quadrature with 51 nodes to evaluate the expectation overεi,t+1QandQto evaluate expectations over the future aggregate state.

The state space of an individual firm consists of the aggregate Markov stateξtand the continuous-valued interest coverage ratioκi,t⁠. We discretizeκon an equally spaced grid with 8,000 points between 0.1 and 200, and use value function iteration to compute the joint fixed point forλs(κi,t,ξt)andλd(κi,t,ξt)based on their equations in the appendix. In doing so, we solve for the optimal aggregate state-dependent thresholdsκD(ξt)⁠,κI(ξt)⁠, andκ¯(ξt)⁠. Because the thresholds are associated with kinks in the value function, the optimization restricts them to grid values ofκ⁠, which makes it essential to rely on a large number of grid points. We use Gaussian quadrature with 51 nodes to evaluate expectations, and linear interpolation to evaluateλtsandλtdat off-grid values ofκthat result from the quadrature. We iterate onλtsandλtduntil the maximum absolute change across all values in the state space falls below10−4for both functions, and the thresholdsκtD⁠,κtI⁠, andκ¯tremain unchanged at their respective grid values.

Given solutions for the equity-to-earnings and debt-to-earnings ratios, CDS and option prices can be evaluated based on the formulae in the appendix. We rely on the same quadrature routine as before to evaluate expectations.

F. Standard Errors

Similar to Nikolov and Whited (2014) , we do not use the optimal weighting matrix in the estimation because covariances are estimated with considerable noise. To account temporal dependence in the data, we use the Newey-West variance-covariance for standard errors. The variance-covariance matrix of the SMM parameter estimate is given by Cov ( θ ^ ) = ( 1 + 1 / H ) ( D ′ W D ) − 1 D ′ W S W D ( D ′ W D ) − 1 / N , where W is the weighting matrix used in the estimation, D the Jacobian matrix, S the Newey-West variance-covariance matrix of the moments with 12 lags, N the sample size, and ( 1 + 1 / H ) accounts for the sample variation of simulations ( Lee and Ingram , 1991 ). The ( i j ) th element of the Jacobian matrix D contains the derivative of model moment i with respect to parameter j ⁠ , D i j = d Ψ i M d θ j ⁠ , which we compute via central finite differences, D i j = Ψ i M ( θ ⊙ ( 1 + e j ϵ ) ) − Ψ i M ( θ ⊙ ( 1 − e j ϵ ) ) 2 θ ϵ , where ⊙ denotes element-by-element multiplication, e j is a column vector with one in position j and zero elsewhere, and ϵ = 0.03 ⁠ .

G. Extended Sample Estimation

In this appendix, we reestimate the model based on the extended 1996–2019 sample by imputing CDS rates for the years 1996–2003 based on corporate bond spreads.

Our choice to base the benchmark estimation on CDS rates was motivated by the fact that corporate bonds feature substantial heterogeneity across firms, for example, in terms of maturities and embedded options, trade fairly irregular due to low liquidity, and potentially contain substantial amounts of noise (see, e.g.,Kelly and Pruitt,2022). In contrast, CDS quotes are standardized across firms and are available on nearly all trading days.

It is also worth noting that data for option prices, leverage, and returns is available for longer samples than CDS rates, and that long-run means of these variables would be estimated more accurately based on the longest available time series for each variable. However, these variables vary systematically over the business cycle and the frequency of recessions differs across subsamples. As a result, such estimates would not be economically consistent with one another. Suppose, for example, that we incorporated leverage data back to 1970 and that average leverage was substantially higher over 1970–2003 than over the 2004–2019 CDS sample. Then average credit spreads would appear unreasonably low given average leverage ratios, but this discrepancy would simply reflect the different sample periods over which the means were estimated. Moments that are estimated over different samples are therefore typically not well-suited for a structural estimation.

G.1 Sample Construction

To extend the sample, we rely on the Mergent FISD database, which contains bond transaction prices reported by insurance companies. From Mergent, we also get information on bond characteristics, including coupon rates, maturities, and other bond features. We focus on senior unsecured U.S. Corporate Debentures with fixed coupon rate, nonconvertible, nonputtable, nonexchangeable, and denominated in U.S. dollars for companies in the S&P 100 index. For each reported transaction, we convert price data into yields and compute credit spreads by subtracting the maturity-matched Treasury yield. For the latter, we linearly interpolate constant-maturity Treasury yields from FRED to the bond’s maturity. To obtain a single monthly observation per firm, we first average credit spreads into a single observation per bond and day, then average daily observations for each bond within each month, and finally average credit spreads and maturities across different bonds of the same firm within each month.

To combine credit spreads with our CDS data, we project CDS rates in the 2004–2019 sample onto bond yields and maturities, 26 ln ⁡ ( 5-year CDS i t ) = β 0 + β 1 ln ⁡ ( yield spread i t ) + β 2 maturity i t + ε i t , compute fitted values based on observations of the explanatory variables over 1996–2003, and add Gaussian noise with the same standard deviation as the regression residual to obtain imputed CDS rates. While option prices for earlier years could in principle be imputed as well, they are considerably more difficult to fit accurately based on observable firm characteristics. We therefore limit the extended sample to the years 1996 and after, for which option prices, returns, and leverage ratios can be observed, and only CDS rates need to be imputed.

Table G.2shows sample moments for the 1996–2019 sample. Relative to the 2004–2019 sample, the most pronounced differences are that leverage ratios are 2 percentage points (12%⁠) higher, and that all variables are somewhat more volatile.

Economically, the model’s fit is comparable to that in the benchmark estimation. Parameter estimates are similar as well, with the exception of bankruptcy costs, which are estimated to be lower in the longer sample. The reason being that the longer sample reflects higher volatility in asset prices, which, by itself, would result in lower leverage ratios. Since leverage is higher in the long sample than the short sample, however, bankruptcy costs need to be lower for the model to match average leverage. The cyclicality of bankruptcy costs is also somewhat larger, which reflects the fact that leverage ratios and asset prices are more volatile in the long sample.
